Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty inconsistent. I'd try to set aside time on weekends and a couple evenings during the week, aiming for maybe 15 hours total, but it rarely worked out that neatly. I found myself doing a lot of reading and note-taking in bursts, often late at night when things were quieter. With 11 online courses to juggle, I had to be strategic about where I put my energy, and this course got attention when exams were coming up more than anything else.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due and how overwhelmed I was feeling. Some weeks I'd be really disciplined, other weeks I'd barely touch the materials until panic set in. Deadlines were the main driver—if something was due, that course got priority. I wish I could say I had a consistent schedule, but life and my other commitments made that really hard to maintain.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I needed to check deadlines, see if there were new announcements, or access specific materials for studying. Sometimes I'd log in just to see what I was behind on, which honestly made me feel worse. The gamified elements they added did motivate me to check in more often, though—seeing progress bars and achievements gave me small wins that helped on tough days.
Q4: For a while, logging in became part of my morning routine with coffee. I'd check the LMS like I check my email, just to stay on top of things. That habit formed during the first few weeks when I was motivated, but it faded when stress piled up and I started feeling sad about everything. The gamified tasks helped bring me back occasionally because they made logging in feel less like a chore.
Q5: The reading materials and written resources were definitely most useful for me since I learn best through reading and writing. I'd download PDFs and annotate them heavily. The discussion posts were helpful too, even though I only contributed a few times—reading what others wrote helped clarify concepts. Videos were okay but I found myself watching them at 2x speed just to get through them.
Q6: I often ignored the optional supplementary videos and some of the peer interaction activities. With moderate anxiety and everything else going on, I had to pick my battles. If something wasn't directly tied to my grade or exam prep, it often got pushed aside. I know that probably hurt my overall learning, but I was just trying to survive and keep my head above water.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to exam performance or if it helped me understand a concept I was struggling with. The gamified elements helped too—earning badges or seeing completion percentages go up gave me a sense of progress even when I felt low. If something felt like busywork with no clear payoff, I'd skip it or do the bare minimum.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like a luxury I couldn't afford. With required work already piling up and my motivation fluctuating, optional stuff got sacrificed first. Required activities had this weight to them—I knew I had to do them, so I'd push through even when I didn't want to. Optional things needed to really grab my interest or offer clear benefits for me to engage with them.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge amount of my studying happened offline. I'd download readings, print them out, and study at the library or at home away from my computer. I have notebooks full of handwritten summaries and practice problems. I also used external resources like YouTube explanations and textbooks I found on my own. The LMS was more of a starting point than where I actually did the deep work.
Q10: There were many nights where I studied for hours from printed materials or my own notes without ever touching the LMS. I'd review concepts, write practice essays, work through problems—all offline. My exam score reflects that effort, but the LMS wouldn't show any of it. Sometimes I'd watch a video once online, take detailed notes, and then study those notes for days without logging back in.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd access a reading or lecture once, download everything I needed, and then work with it offline for weeks. My approach was to gather materials in one session and then process them on my own time. This meant my LMS activity looked minimal even during periods of intense studying. It was more efficient for me to work offline where I could focus without distractions.
Q12: There were definitely periods where engagement became really hard. When I was feeling particularly sad or when anxiety spiked, just opening the LMS felt overwhelming. Around midterms, when everything piled up at once, I withdrew for a bit. My resilience isn't great, so setbacks hit me harder than they probably should. Those were the times my attendance and participation really suffered.
Q13: Definitely. When I was fatigued or stressed, I'd log into the LMS but just stare at the screen without really absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities from my other courses meant I had to constantly triage. Sometimes I'd open materials with good intentions but feel so overwhelmed that I'd close everything and try again later. The cognitive load of managing 11 courses was honestly brutal at times.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd scroll through materials or watch videos while my mind was completely elsewhere. I'd technically be 'engaging' according to the system, but I wasn't actually learning anything. These were usually low mood days when I felt like I should be doing something productive but couldn't actually focus. I'd feel guilty afterwards for wasting time.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, even knowing they were important. My assignment completion rate was pretty bad, honestly. Sometimes it was anxiety—the task felt too big to start. Other times I was just sad and couldn't muster the energy. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and tomorrow would come with the same feelings. It's frustrating because I knew better.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. I'd have bursts of intense LMS activity right before something was due, then go quiet again. My engagement graph probably looks like a series of spikes rather than a steady line. Without deadlines, I'd drift. The gamified tasks helped a bit because they created mini-deadlines and goals, but major deadlines still drove most of my behavior.
Q17: Feedback definitely affected me, sometimes too much. Positive feedback would boost my motivation for a few days. Negative or critical feedback would send me into a spiral where I'd avoid the course for a while. With my moderate anxiety, I'd sometimes delay checking grades because I was afraid of what I'd see. When I did get good feedback, it reminded me I was capable, which helped during low periods.
Q18: The course structure was a mixed bag. Having clear modules and deadlines helped me know what to prioritize. But the sheer volume of content across all my courses made it hard to keep up with everything. The gamified elements were actually helpful—they broke things into smaller chunks that felt more manageable. Without that structure, I probably would have fallen further behind.
Q19: The gamified tasks made engagement easier because they gave me small, achievable goals when everything else felt overwhelming. What made it harder was when too many things were due at once or when instructions were unclear. Also, the discussion requirements felt forced sometimes—I'd post just to meet requirements rather than because I had something meaningful to say. More flexibility would have helped.
Q20: I think that pattern makes sense when students are anxious or uncertain about what they're supposed to do. They might be logging in repeatedly, reviewing materials, trying to understand requirements, but struggling to actually produce work. Fear of failure can make you prepare endlessly without ever feeling ready to submit. I've been there—high activity doesn't mean high confidence or productivity.
Q21: That actually describes me pretty well. My exam score was really high despite my LMS activity being moderate at best. I did most of my real learning offline—reading, writing notes, practicing. The LMS was just where I got materials, not where I learned. Some students are just more efficient with their online time, or they have study methods that don't require constant platform engagement.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, how long you spend. But they miss the quality of engagement and all the offline work. They can't tell if you're actually learning or just scrolling mindlessly. They definitely can't capture emotional state—whether you're engaged because you're interested or just anxious about failing. The numbers tell a very incomplete story.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions. It's when I'm curious, asking questions in my head, making connections to other things I know. It's not about time spent logged in—it's about mental investment. Some of my most engaged moments happened alone with a textbook, far from any learning platform. True engagement is internal.
Q24: The LMS can't show the hours I spent with printed readings at the library, the conversations with classmates outside of the discussion board, or the YouTube videos I watched to supplement lectures. It can't show that some days I was too sad to engage meaningfully even when I logged in. It also can't capture that despite struggling with motivation and mood, I genuinely cared about learning and pushed through to earn my distinction.
